  see two different blobs, the edit operations that produced them are gone. Every product
  below is, at bottom, a different answer to "what do you retain so that merge is possible,
  and at what granularity?"

  How the big products solve it

  Dropbox / Google Drive desktop sync (opaque files): detect, never merge, never lose. They
  don't understand file internals, so on concurrent edits they keep one version as canonical
  and write the other to Document (conflicted copy 2026-06-02).md. Crude, but it has two
  properties your current code lacks: it's never lossy and never sticky. Your conflict state
  is strictly worse than Dropbox's — it wedges and surfaces nothing actionable.

  Google Docs native (OT — Operational Transform): a central server serializes every
  keystroke. Each edit is an operation against a base revision; the server transforms
  concurrent ops so they commute. This is what makes real-time co-editing work, but it
  requires (a) a always-on mediator and (b) edits expressed as operations, not file saves.
  Your "poll the file every 1.5s" model is the opposite of this. Not applicable.

  Newer collaborative editors (Notion-ish, Linear, Figma — CRDTs, e.g. Yjs/Automerge):
  structure the doc so concurrent edits always converge with no coordinator. Mathematically
  the cleanest answer to multi-writer-no-lock. But the document stops being "a markdown file
  / a TEXT column" and becomes a CRDT structure you serialize. For a tool where the two
  writers are one agent and one human occasionally editing a file, that's enormous overkill —
  you'd pay CRDT representation costs to solve a 2-writer, low-frequency race.

  git: three-way merge against a common ancestor (diff3). This is the one that actually fits
  you, because git solves exactly your shape: two divergent snapshots plus a known merge
  base. With the ancestor, non-overlapping edits auto-merge silently and only
  genuinely-overlapping hunks become conflicts. The whole "agent rewrote section 3 while the
  human fixed a typo in section 1" case — which is most of your real conflicts — just
  resolves.

  What I'd actually do here (and why it's not a bandaid)

  The key realization: murder already stores almost everything a 3-way merge needs.

  - last_materialized_hash is already your merge-base pointer.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
❯ 
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ⏵⏵ bypass permissions on (shift+tab to cycle) · esc to interrupt · ↓ to manage

  ● main                                                        ↑/↓ to select · Enter to view
  ◯ general-purpose  Reconcile conflicted plan                           52s · ↓ 23.1k tokens
